--- format: revealjs: theme: ../../styles/meds-slides-styles.scss slide-number: true chalkboard: true title-slide: false jupyter: eds232-env --- ## {#title-slide data-menu-title="Title Slide" background="#053660"} [EDS 232]{.custom-title}
[Lesson 4]{.custom-subtitle} [*Multiple linear regression*]{.custom-subtitle} --- ## {#in-this-lesson data-menu-title="In this lesson"} [In this lesson]{.slide-title}

- The multiple linear regression (MLR) model - Estimating coefficients with least squares - Individual $p$-values: partial effects - Comparing MLR to individual SLRs - Hypothesis testing: the $F$-statistic - Model fit: $R^2$ in MLR and adjusted $R^2$ - Variable selection: forward, backward, and mixed --- ## {#section-mlr data-menu-title="# The MLR model #" background="#047C90"}

The multiple linear regression model

--- ## {#mlr-motivation data-menu-title="From SLR to MLR"} [From SLR to MLR]{.slide-title}
In SLR we modelled the relationship between **one** predictor and a response: $$\texttt{co2\_reduction} \approx \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 \cdot \texttt{process}$$ . . .
But the GreenAbate dataset has **three** potential predictors. Running separate SLRs has two problems: ::: {.body-text-m} - No single prediction combining all three predictors - Each SLR ignores the other predictors — coefficients can be misleading when predictors are correlated ::: . . .
::: {.center-text .teal-text .body-text-m} **Multiple linear regression** handles all predictors simultaneously. ::: --- ## {#mlr-model data-menu-title="The MLR model"} [The MLR model]{.slide-title}
With $p$ predictors $X_1, \ldots, X_p$ and response $Y$: $$Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p + \epsilon$$ . . .
::: {.body-text-m} The key phrase in interpreting each coefficient: > *$\hat{\beta}_j$ is the average change in $Y$ associated with a one-unit increase in $X_j$, **holding all other predictors constant**.* ::: . . .
For GreenAbate ($p = 3$): $$\texttt{co2\_reduction} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \texttt{process} + \beta_2 \cdot \texttt{efficiency} + \beta_3 \cdot \texttt{offsets} + \epsilon$$ --- ## {#mlr-fit data-menu-title="MLR fitted coefficients"} [Fitted MLR coefficients for GreenAbate]{.slide-title}
::: {#d26a3d57 .cell execution_count=2} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Coefficient Std. error t-statistic p-value Intercept 3.4543 0.3416 10.11 < 0.0001 process 0.0461 0.0013 35.37 < 0.0001 efficiency 0.1831 0.0075 24.37 < 0.0001 offsets -0.0088 0.0037 -2.36 0.0193 ``` ::: ::: . . .
::: {.body-text-m} The fitted model is: $$\widehat{\texttt{co2}} = 3\.454 + 0\.0461 \cdot \texttt{process} + 0\.1831 \cdot \texttt{efficiency} \-0\.0088 \cdot \texttt{offsets}$$ ::: --- ## {#checkin-mlr-interp data-menu-title="Check-in: MLR coefficients" background="#053660"}

Check-in

What is the interpretation of the `efficiency` coefficient of 0\.1831?

A firm spends \$100K on process, \$20K on efficiency, \$50K on offsets. What is the predicted CO₂ reduction?

--- ## {#checkin-mlr-answers data-menu-title="Check-in: MLR answers" background="#053660"}

Check-in — answers

Each additional \$1K in efficiency upgrades is associated with 0\.1831 ktCO₂e of additional CO₂ reduction, holding process and offset spending constant.

$\hat{y} = 3\.454 + 0\.0461 \times 100 + 0\.1831 \times 20 \-0\.0088 \times 50 \approx 11\.29$ ktCO₂e

--- ## {#section-pvalues data-menu-title="# p-values for coefficients #" background="#047C90"}

p-values for the coefficients

--- ## {#pvalues-mlr data-menu-title="Individual p-values in MLR"} [Individual $p$-values: partial effects]{.slide-title}
In SLR, the $p$-value for $\hat{\beta}_1$ tests $H_0: \beta_1 = 0$ — is $X$ related to $Y$? . . .
In MLR, the $p$-value for each $\hat{\beta}_j$ tests the **partial effect** of $X_j$: ::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text} *Does $X_j$ add information beyond what the other predictors already explain?* ::: . . .
::: {.body-text-m} - Small $p$-value for $\hat{\beta}_j$ → $X_j$ is informative **given the other predictors are in the model** - Large $p$-value for $\hat{\beta}_j$ → $X_j$ adds little beyond what the others already capture ::: --- ## {#checkin-offsets-pvalue data-menu-title="Check-in: offsets p-value" background="#053660"}

Check-in

The `offsets` variable has a p-value of 0\.0193 in the MLR.

Does this mean carbon offset purchases have no relationship with CO₂ reductions?

What exactly does this p-value tell us?

--- ## {#checkin-offsets-answers data-menu-title="Check-in: offsets answers" background="#053660"}

Check-in — answers

Not necessarily. A large p-value means `offsets` adds little beyond what `process` and `efficiency` already explain.

In a separate SLR of `co2_reduction` on `offsets` alone, the result could differ.

Here, the true coefficient for `offsets` is $-0.001$ (near zero), so the model is correctly detecting it contributes almost nothing.

--- ## {#section-comparison data-menu-title="# MLR vs. individual SLRs #" background="#047C90"}

Comparing MLR to individual SLRs

--- ## {#slr-vs-mlr data-menu-title="SLR vs. MLR coefficients"} [SLR vs. MLR: why not run separate regressions?]{.slide-title}
::: {#5630eef9 .cell execution_count=3} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Predictor SLR coeff SLR p-value MLR coeff MLR p-value process 0.0460 < 0.0001 0.0461 < 0.0001 efficiency 0.1793 < 0.0001 0.1831 < 0.0001 offsets -0.0060 0.6153 -0.0088 0.0193 ``` ::: ::: . . .
::: {.body-text-m} - In this dataset, SLR ≈ MLR coefficients — because the predictors were drawn independently (uncorrelated by construction). - In real data, predictors are typically correlated. The divergence can be large, sometimes **reversing the sign** of a coefficient. - The MLR coefficient reflects the *direct* effect of each predictor, holding the others constant. ::: --- ## {#section-fstat data-menu-title="# Hypothesis testing #" background="#047C90"}

Hypothesis testing: the F-statistic

--- ## {#hypotheses-mlr data-menu-title="Overall test in MLR"} [Is any predictor related to the response?]{.slide-title}
In SLR we tested $H_0: \beta_1 = 0$. In MLR, the overall question is: $$H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = \cdots = \beta_p = 0$$ $$H_a: \text{at least one } \beta_j \neq 0$$ . . .
We test this with the **$F$-statistic**: $$F = \frac{(TSS - RSS)/p}{RSS/(n - p - 1)}$$ . . . ::: {.body-text-m} - $F \approx 1$: no predictor is related to $Y$ (consistent with $H_0$) - $F \gg 1$: at least one predictor explains variance beyond noise → evidence against $H_0$ ::: --- ## {#fstat-greenabate data-menu-title="F-statistic: GreenAbate"} [F-statistic for the GreenAbate MLR]{.slide-title}
::: {#5d41c2ba .cell execution_count=4} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Quantity Value F-statistic 612.13 p-value < 0.0001 ``` ::: ::: . . .
::: {.body-text-m} An $F$-statistic of 612\.1 with $p \ll 0.05$ gives very strong evidence that at least one predictor is associated with CO₂ reductions. ::: . . .
**Why not just use the individual p-values?** When $p$ is large, some individual p-values will be small *by chance* even when no predictor is truly related to $Y$. With $p = 100$ truly null predictors, we expect ~5 to have $p < 0.05$ by random chance alone. The $F$-statistic tests all coefficients jointly and accounts for this. --- ## {#section-modelfit data-menu-title="# Model fit #" background="#047C90"}

Model fit: R² in MLR

--- ## {#r2-mlr data-menu-title="R² in MLR"} [$R^2$ in multiple regression]{.slide-title}
The $R^2$ formula is the same as in SLR: $$R^2 = 1 - \frac{RSS}{TSS}$$ . . .
::: {.body-text-m .teal-text} **Important caveat:** adding a predictor to an MLR model will **always** increase $R^2$ (or leave it unchanged), even if that predictor has no real relationship with $Y$. ::: . . .
The **adjusted $R^2$** penalizes for the number of predictors: $$\bar{R}^2 = 1 - \frac{RSS/(n-p-1)}{TSS/(n-1)}$$ Adjusted $R^2$ can **decrease** when an irrelevant predictor is added — making it better for comparing models of different sizes. --- ## {#r2-comparison data-menu-title="R² comparison: SLR vs MLR"} [Comparing SLR and MLR model fit]{.slide-title}
::: {#0020a585 .cell execution_count=5} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Model R² RSE (ktCO₂e) SLR (process only) 0.611 3.138 MLR (all three predictors) 0.904 1.571 ``` ::: ::: . . .
::: {.body-text-m} - $R^2$ increased from 0\.611 to 0\.904 — `efficiency` is genuinely predictive. - RSE decreased from 3\.138 to 1\.571 ktCO₂e — predictions are more accurate with all three predictors. ::: --- ## {#section-varsel data-menu-title="# Variable selection #" background="#047C90"}

Variable selection

--- ## {#varsel-problem data-menu-title="The variable selection problem"} [Which predictors should we include?]{.slide-title}
With $p$ predictors there are $2^p$ possible subsets: ::: {.body-text-m} - $p = 3$: **8 models** — feasible to compare exhaustively - $p = 10$: 1,024 models - $p = 30$: over 1 billion models — completely infeasible ::: . . .
Three common automated approaches for large $p$: ::: {.body-text-m} - [**Forward selection**]{.teal-text}: start with nothing, add one predictor at a time - [**Backward selection**]{.teal-text}: start with everything, remove one at a time - [**Mixed selection**]{.teal-text}: combine forward and backward steps ::: --- ## {#all-8-models data-menu-title="All 8 GreenAbate models"} [RSS for all 8 possible GreenAbate models]{.slide-title}
::: {#85ce90d5 .cell execution_count=6} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Predictors RSS (intercept only) 5016.8 process 1950.3 efficiency 3601.4 offsets 5010.4 process + efficiency 497.5 process + offsets 1949.8 efficiency + offsets 3571.4 process + efficiency + offsets 483.8 ``` ::: ::: . . .
::: {.teal-text .body-text-m} **Check-in:** Trace through forward selection using this table. Which predictor would be added first? Second? Does adding `offsets` meaningfully reduce RSS? ::: --- ## {#checkin-forward-sel data-menu-title="Check-in: forward selection" background="#053660"}

Check-in — forward selection answers

Step 1: `process` has the lowest single-predictor RSS → added first.

Step 2: `process + efficiency` has lower RSS than `process + offsets` → `efficiency` added second.

Step 3: adding `offsets` gives only a tiny further reduction — whether it is included depends on the stopping criterion.

--- ## {#forward-backward data-menu-title="Forward and backward selection"} [Forward and backward selection]{.slide-title}
:::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} **Forward selection** 1. Start: null model (intercept only) 2. Add the predictor with the largest RSS reduction 3. Repeat until stopping criterion is met ✓ Always applicable (works when $p > n$) ✗ A variable added early may become redundant later ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} **Backward selection** 1. Start: full model (all $p$ predictors) 2. Remove the predictor with the largest $p$-value 3. Repeat until all remaining predictors are significant ✓ Considers all predictors from the start ✗ Cannot be used when $p > n$ ::: :::: --- ## {#mixed-selection data-menu-title="Mixed selection"} [Mixed selection]{.slide-title}
**Mixed selection** (stepwise) combines forward and backward steps:
::: {.body-text-m} 1. Start with the null model 2. **Forward step**: add the variable with the largest RSS reduction 3. **Backward step**: check if any included variable now has a large $p$-value — if so, remove it 4. Continue until no variables are added or removed ::: . . .
::: {.body-text-m .teal-text} This fixes the main weakness of forward selection: a variable added early can be *removed* once other, better variables are in the model. ::: --- ## {#other-metrics data-menu-title="Other selection criteria"} [Other criteria for variable selection]{.slide-title}
RSS alone always favors larger models — use criteria that **penalize complexity**:
::: {.body-text-m} | Criterion | Formula | Goal | |-----------|---------|------| | Adjusted $R^2$ | $1 - \frac{RSS/(n-p-1)}{TSS/(n-1)}$ | Maximize | | AIC | $n\log(RSS/n) + 2p$ | Minimize | | BIC | $n\log(RSS/n) + p\log(n)$ | Minimize | | Test MSE (CV) | held-out set error | Minimize | ::: . . .
::: {.body-text-s} BIC penalizes extra predictors more heavily than AIC (for $n > 7$), tending to select sparser models. Cross-validation is the most direct approach when the goal is prediction accuracy. ::: --- ## {#recap data-menu-title="Recap"} [In this lesson we covered]{.slide-title}
- **MLR model**: $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p + \epsilon$ — coefficients interpreted *holding all else constant* - Least squares minimizes RSS; computer solves it - **Individual p-values**: test partial effects (does $X_j$ add beyond the others?) - **F-statistic**: tests $H_0: \beta_1 = \cdots = \beta_p = 0$ — use before relying on individual p-values - **$R^2$** always increases with predictors; use **adjusted $R^2$** to compare models of different sizes - **Variable selection**: forward, backward, mixed — use AIC, BIC, adjusted $R^2$, or CV as criteria
